data.title: Aura Minerals: The Real Gold Is Here
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying shares in the mining company Aura Minerals (NASDAQ:AUGO). This is my first article about the company, however I have several articles with assertive recommendations on gold and gold mining ETFs. My intention in this article is to base my recommendation through an in-depth study of the company, its history, business model, strategy, finances and valuation.

History

The gold mining company was founded in Canada in 1946, and in 2006 it was listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange. The company went through ups and downs from 2008 to 2016, facing a financial crisis, high mining costs, investments in new projects, and a sharp drop in the price of copper.

In 2016 when prices were at their lowest, the Brazilian investment fund Northwestern Enterprises took control of the company and in 2018 began a broad turnaround process to put the company back on track for growth. The restructuring culminated in its double listing: In addition to Canada, the company opened its capital on the Brazilian Stock Exchange, B3 (BOLSY), in 2019. Between 2020 and 2025, the company went through a growth phase with the maturation of projects combined with the soaring price of gold.

In July 2025, the company listed its shares on Nasdaq with plans to move from being a junior gold miner to reaching its large global peers. This allowed ETF managers to become shareholders. Aura Minerals is part of the VanEck Junior Gold Miners (NYSEARCA:GDXJ) ETF for example.

Corporate profile

Since the start of its restructuring in 2018, the company has grown from two main operations to six projects in three countries and prospects for growth with developments. The map below consolidates the mines in operation and projects under development.

The company's revenues in 2025 came 82% from gold, 16% from copper and 2% from others (silver and molybdenum). Its main mines are MSG, Borborema, Almas, Minosa, Apoema and Aranzazu. The detail is that only the Aranzazu mine contains metals other than gold such as cooper, silver and molybdenum, all the others are pure gold mines. It is interesting that the company uses the term "GEO" and it means "Gold Equivalent Ounce", that is the company converts all other metals into gold, which makes the investor's analysis easier.

As we saw above, current production is approximately 280K GEO. The next relevant project is Era Dorada, and its full operation is scheduled for 2028. The potential is for 100K oz of production. The company also secured the eighth mine, Matupá, which, although licensed, is not yet incorporated into the models. Given all these projects, the company aims to produce 600K GEO in the future.

Financial analysis

The company has an A grade in Profitability Grade. Aura has a high gross profit margin (+58%) and high EBITDA margin (+58%) both surpassing the company's and the sector's historical figures. These two indicators are the most important in the analysis of miners since the net income margin can transmit inaccurate information due to the financial result (often contaminated by hedges and exchange rate variation).

The company also has an A rating in Growth Grade with strong growth in all indicators driven by greater production and soaring gold prices.

When we analyze it against junior gold mining peers, the company also stands out in terms of margins and revenue growth over the last 5 years. Negative net income margin is the main discrepancy again but it can also be an excellent trigger in the future when it becomes positive again.

A major attraction is the company's operational leverage. Aura Minerals keeps costs stable and is able to greatly increase margins due to the soaring price of gold. An interesting fact is the sequential increase in production from the first half of 2025.

After all this explanation it is possible to conclude that the company is a successful turnaround. Management strategies were impeccable not only in improving operational efficiency but also in increasing the liquidity of shares and making them accessible to global investors. So the rise in gold was the icing on the cake, but is the stock still worth buying?

Valuation via P/NAV

I will analyze the valuation analysis using the most common metric for the sector: P/NAV. As we can see below Aura has a discount compared to relevant gold miners in the sector, but also compared to junior gold miners.

The average P/NAV for junior gold miners is 1.04x NAV, and for relevant mining companies in the sector it is 1.21x NAV. This means that if the company only traded at the average of each pair, we would have 30% upside and 51% upside respectively. Of course a trigger is necessary for re-rating, but I'll talk about that later. Now I will analyze it through a DCF.

Valuation via DCF

As a reference, the company reported at the end of 2025 a gold sales volume of 195,968 oz at a price of $3,446/oz, which results in $675 million.

The SA indicates that in 2025 the company reported $921.7 million in revenue, therefore it is possible to infer that other metals (in addition to gold) contributed $246.4 million. The company also indicates that the total sales volume in 2025 was 278,296 GEO, therefore the sales volume of other metals was 82,328 and its sales price was $2,993/GEO. This information will be useful for our valuation model.

For valuation I will use a 5 Year DCF Model Growth Exit. The company is expected to reach production of 296 thousand oz in 2026, and will grow to 520 thousand oz by 2030 (silver, copper and molybdenum will close the gap to 600K GEO, which is the company's goal).

My projections for the price of gold start at $5000 in 2026 and end at $4300 in 2030. The EBITDA margin will rise to 60% in 2026, and I project it to be stable until 2029, the following year it will reach 65% due to efficiencies from new projects.

I estimated an increase in CapEx in 2026 and 2027 reducing until 2030 as projects mature. The discount rate is 9.5%, which in my opinion well prices the risk of a junior gold miner, and the growth in perpetuity is close to zero to portray the useful life of the mines and the possibility of new projects. The fair value is $138 a 40% upside to the current price.

Why do I consider my assumptions to be very conservative? My estimates indicate that the company will reach the 600K GEO production target only in 2030, but the speed of improvements suggests that this milestone could occur much sooner. Not to mention the gold assumptions, since I have articles that indicate the fair value of gold at $6000/oz and if I used that target in the valuation, the upside would be even greater. The complete valuation is below:

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Aura Minerals (NASDAQ:AUGO) shares based on the 40% upside compared to the current price through DCF, and the potential re-rating of the P/NAV. I am extremely confident in the recommendation as I see that SA's powerful Quant tools recommend Strong Buy.

But as we know for a re-rating to occur, a trigger is necessary, and in this case there is a very clear trigger. As I said the company is already part of the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDXJ), which currently has $10 billion in AUM, however, the gain in liquidity and rise in Aura Minerals shares should allow it to join the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSE:GDX) soon.

The company already meets revenue requirements (more than 50% coming from gold), market cap (above $150 million). If the sequence of increases continues there is the possibility of the company meeting the ADTV requirements (above $1 million in the last 3 months) and possibly entering the universe of shares eligible for the ETF in the 1st half of the year.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Among the more general risks, the company is very exposed to gold price volatility. Not to mention that operations can be affected by adverse weather conditions, regulatory changes, and various operational problems.

Although the company has evolved, it is still not comparable to large companies in the gold sector, given its smaller size, lower liquidity and higher risk profile. Speaking of higher risks, I will address your most recent operational report, the Q1 production report, and comment a little more on operational risks.

Latest Earnings Results and More Risks

The company produced 82,100 GEO in Q1, an increase of 37% compared to the last quarter. Although production was a record, when annualized, the performance was below the lower limit of the guidance for 2026. This means that the company started the year at a pace that is still insufficient to signal greater comfort in meeting the annual target.

Among the assets that contributed most, the MSG and Borborena operations were the main positive highlights. The production was about 50% better than my projections. Aranzazu, Almas and Minosa were as expected, however, Apoena was around 15% below my projections.

In recent quarters, the company has only delivered what the market expected. The next results will be released on May 6th and I believe that the company will once again deliver what was expected, but perhaps there is a "positive surprise" missing here.

This is the main risk for the thesis, and also a challenge for management. Although robust operational improvements have occurred, investors are concerned that results have improved much more due to the soaring price of gold than due to the company's efficiency. In my opinion, this is the main reason behind the discount in valuation compared to peers.

The Bottom Line

In this article I had the opportunity to do an extremely in-depth study of a gold mining company with little visibility in SA. Despite the shares rising almost 100% this year, to date, there are only 16 articles about the company in SA.

Aura Minerals has a history marked by a successful turnaround, which was catalyzed by the spike in gold prices. The valuation was analyzed using three metrics: P/NAV, DCF and SA's Quant tools, and in all of them the perception is that the upside is still very large.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Aura Minerals shares. The company's investment thesis brings together what few other theses can: strong momentum, profitability, growth, attractive valuation and clear triggers for a re-rating.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Vaalco Energy: Hitting The Jackpot
data.description:
Since my last article on Vaalco Energy (EGY), the company has continued the progress of the production build (or production increases) from the production low point due to routine maintenance and inspections, repairs, and more that are part of the industry but affect a small company like this because it is not large enough to be diversified. This article follows the climb in production through (in this case) another discovery well that was successful and the progress of the FPSO repairs (and of course coming back to the field).

The thing to note about the Gabon drilling campaign - which just announced another successful well that flows nearly 5000 BOD (of which this company nets about 58%) - is that this was a five-well campaign originally with an option for another five wells. We will not know management's decision on the option part for some time yet because the first five wells are not completed. Also, management has to evaluate the geologic information collected as a result of those five wells. However, at this point it does appear that some of those optional wells will be drilled. At a time of high oil prices, this is just a decision to print more money the way things are going.

Back in November, I wrote that this is one of those "guaranteed" plays (if that is even possible in the upstream world) because the shutdown of a field for inspection while an FPSO was being repaired (and more) would all be overcome. These were largely routine things with (probably) small probabilities of going wrong. But more importantly, the company was going into this with a debt-free balance sheet. Therefore, there were a whole lot of options as to how to manage these relatively large projects successfully.

Stock Price

The Iranian situation that caused commodity prices to soar is "icing on the cake" considering that the stock price looked like the production was never coming back.

The thing to realize is that the stock price got to panic levels back in November as if both routine and good news from what was about to happen were not possible. Instead, the stock was priced as though that missing production was not coming back (or commodity prices were going through the floor). Such pessimism is almost always followed by what is happening now.

Even now, the FPSO is back in its respective field, and the hooking-up process has begun with a projected start before the end of the second quarter. Between the drilling campaign and the startup of the FPSO, the second half of the year is going to show some big production gains compared to the second half of fiscal year 2025, when much of this was offline at some point and the drilling campaign had not begun.

More importantly, the company kept investors informed about how the Egyptian accounts receivable improved through this whole situation. That added badly needed cash by the tens of millions of dollars at a time when that cash did a whole lot of good.

Some will point to the "luck" of higher commodity prices. It is true that higher commodity prices were lucky. But this management was busy making its own luck even if higher commodity prices never happened. That is the key in this business. There was no dependency on the Iranian situation to earn more money. Instead, that is a very appreciated extra.

From Quarter 4 To Quarter 1

The quarterly report of the first quarter is going to be reporting continuing operational improvement from the fourth quarter as things come back online. As the stock price reaction above demonstrates, Mr. Market had no idea there would ever be good news on either the exploration front or with the routine restoration of production.

More importantly, fields keep switching from negative cash flow (for routine items) back to regular production (with positive cash flow). Now the market is focused on that positive aspect.

With that in mind, let us see where exactly the first quarter is starting from:

The fourth quarter likewise benefitted from rising production. But as shown above, the first quarter begins with the exit production rate (not the fourth quarter average rate). It is very possible that with the hookup of the FPSO completed by the end of the second quarter, the company will exit the second quarter somewhere near 30,000 BOED (mostly oil) at a time of very decent commodity prices.

To me, the stock price is nothing close to estimating that kind of production. With still more wells to be drilled, the good news is likely to keep coming to push the stock price higher. Of course there will be some disappointments along the way. But the successes so far are much more important than any possible disappointments.

More importantly, any reported losses will likely end when the FPSO begins production. The drilling campaign success combined with the higher-than-expected commodity prices may enable a sooner return to profitability.

More importantly, all this good news means that the company will likely end the situation (of losses) with a whole lot less debt than was originally expected. That may be good news for the future of the dividend.

Risks

This company does business in Western Africa. It is therefore not affected by the Iranian situation (and can therefore "cash in" on the situation). However, the stability where it operates is another matter entirely and has been documented by me in past articles. So far, no matter what happens, every regime that comes in (in the area) has supported this industry. But there is no assurance that will continue. Right now, the market attention has shifted from this issue. But it likely will be back near the top of concerns in the future if the past is any guide.

At some point, oil prices will decline. But in this case, unless that decline is severe and sustained, this company has growth plans that will likely mitigate the downside of declining prices. It needs to be understood that offshore plans rarely happen unless profitability is extremely good because paybacks are often longer than onshore. The way that the risk of low commodity prices is covered is by not drilling unless projected low breakeven points are a "given."

A management that builds a company in an unstable area like Western Africa is likely a very small subset of all the managements out there. Therefore, a loss of services could prove to be a material setback for this company. Replacing such a loss could prove to be a real challenge.

Summary

Small companies often have more volatile stock prices simply because events that are not all that material for larger producers often have a very material effect on quarterly results. In this case, the pessimism got way overdone last summer (and into the winter).

One of the things that kept risk to a minimum was that the operator of the field for the FPSO undergoing repairs and upgrades was Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). That should have provided quite a bit more assurance that things that tend to go wrong with a smaller operator had a lot less chance of happening with an operator of that stature.

But in this relatively frothy market, nothing hurts like negative comparisons. So, when the FPSO went offline, negative earnings comparisons were almost assured, and the stock price headed South. When the Gabon platform then was shut down for routine maintenance and inspections, the market got even more upset as shown on the stock price chart.

But the odds of a catastrophic outcome that the market feared were very low (for the upstream business).

Now the stock price is responding to a lot of routine restoration of production and of course good exploration results as though none of that could happen. The stock price is up nicely and is likely to go higher as the good news continues to arrive.

The Gabon good news is far more likely to happen because that production area has been around for a long time. Therefore, exploration risk is significantly lower than might otherwise be expected. The new leases that the company has obtained will likely have much higher exploration risk until management determines what is reasonably known and what is pure speculation.

But this company overall has a very good record finding oil. I would expect some disappointments along the way. But that is entirely normal. Overall, I think the goal to triple production over the next five years is likely intact.

I also think that even if commodity prices retreat at some point, the growth plans provide considerable downside protection. This is one very profitable little company.

data.title: SMLF: A Multi-Factor Small-Cap ETF With Quality And Cyclical Tilts
data.description:
The iShares U.S. Small-Cap Equity Factor ETF (SMLF) multi-factor design gives the fund an edge within the small-cap segment, particularly relative to the Russell 2000, which is reflected in better valuation and profitability metrics. This appears to have been reflected in a consistent outperformance within the group over time, as highlighted in my previous article covering the fund in early 2025. While the fund maintains a sector mix relatively close to the benchmark, its increased cyclical exposure represents an opportunity for upside but also poses an additional downside risk amid an uneven macro backdrop for small caps. In this context, the next sections will detail the fund’s core holdings and compare its sector mix, valuation, and performance with the Russell 2000 and small-cap peers.

ETF Description and Highlights

SMLF provides exposure primarily to U.S. small-cap companies that exhibit attractive characteristics based on a multi-factor approach. The fund follows a rules-based structure, tracking the STOXX U.S. Small-Cap Equity Factor Index. Starting from an initial universe of companies that correspond to the bottom 15% of the U.S. equity market, the index’s methodology applies a multi-factor screening and optimization process, including momentum, quality, value, and low volatility indicators. These factors are measured using a combination of signals, including price performance, earnings growth, and revisions, profitability metrics, valuation ratios, and historical price volatility, along with ESG indicators such as carbon intensity and emissions targets.

Companies are scored and ranked according to this multi-factor model, which, as the next step, employs an optimization process that balances factor exposure so that sector and risk characteristics remain aligned with a parent market-cap-weighted index, while also controlling stock and sector concentration, as individual holdings are subject to caps, such as a maximum weight of 10%. The index is also reviewed and rebalanced on a quarterly basis to reflect changes in market conditions.

As of April 10, 2026, SMLF had $3.5 billion in assets under management, invested in 891 companies, with an average market capitalization of $6.72 billion. The fund maintains a clear tilt toward smaller market segments, with small caps accounting for 53.3% of total assets and micro caps at 18.7%. This is a picture that is largely unchanged compared to early 2025, when small and micro caps made up 51.1% and 19.4% of the fund, respectively. That said, SMLF maintains a relatively higher average market cap than the Russell 2000, a widely adopted benchmark for small caps and here represented by the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), which has an average market cap of $3.39 million, nearly half that of SMLF.

SMLF’s top ten holdings are EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME) (1.24%), nVent Electric plc (NVT) (0.81%), Jabil Inc. (JBL) (0.81%), Interactive Brokers Group, Inc. (IBKR) (0.77%), Tapestry, Inc. (TPR) (0.70%), Mueller Industries, Inc. (MLI) (0.65%), Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL) (0.63%), Flex Ltd. (FLEX) (0.61%), Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) (0.54%), and United Therapeutics Corporation (UTHR) (0.53%), which together account for only 7% of the fund, highlighting the fund’s diversified structure. Still, top holdings are somewhat concentrated in a few sectors, such as industrials, technology, and consumer discretionary, suggesting a portfolio that leans toward cyclical sectors, at least within its largest positions.

The following table presents a group of small-cap funds, ranging from traditional market-cap-weighted approaches to factor-driven strategies, that will serve as the peer group for SMLF in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB), the largest fund in the group, which follows an index that uses a straightforward market-cap-weighted methodology. Next, the Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF (SCHA) follows a similar approach, replicating the Dow Jones U.S. Small-Cap Total Stock Market Index with broad exposure across small and micro-cap stocks. The iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) adds a layer of quality, screening by profitability measures, while maintaining a market-cap-weighting structure. Moving into multifactor funds, the John Hancock Multifactor Small Cap ETF (JHSC) combines filters for valuation and profitability, with a modified market-cap weighting methodology, while the First Trust Small Cap Core AlphaDEX ETF (FYX) tracks an index that ranks stocks based on fundamentals, such as growth and value, applying a weighting process that results in a strong tilt toward micro-cap names. Lastly, the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta U.S. Small Cap Equity ETF (GSSC) blends multiple factors, including value, momentum, quality, and low volatility, applied to an initial universe composed of the Russell 2000 universe, also with a greater exposure to the lower end of the market capitalization spectrum.

From a sector allocation perspective, SMLF is led by industrials at 19.8%, with technology and financial services close behind at 15.3% and 15.0%, respectively. Healthcare and consumer discretionary are still relevant exposures, accounting for 13.4% and 12.1% of the portfolio, while real estate (5.7%), basic materials (4.8%), energy (4.7%), consumer staples (3.7%), communication services (3.1%), and utilities (2.4%) complement the portfolio. This marks a slight increase in cyclical areas like industrials (+2.7%) and basic materials (+1.4%), as well as healthcare (+1.9%), from the sector allocation picture in February 2025, while the reduction in technology (-3.9%) and consumer discretionary (-2.6%), suggests a more cautious stance in growth segments for the time being.

Given SMLF’s sector mix, the fund appears overweight in consumer discretionary (+3.5%), industrials (+2.5%), and consumer staples (+1.3%) relative to the Russell 2000, which is offset by underweight allocations in healthcare (-4.1%), financial services (-1.0%), and energy (-1.0%). As we can see, aside from a few more pronounced sector deviations, such as in consumer discretionary and healthcare, SMLF’s sector mix remains broadly in line with a market-cap-weighted index like the Russell 2000.

Breaking down SMLF’s positions across its largest sectors, we see that in industrials the fund mixes exposure across a few core segments, including construction & engineering, machinery, aerospace & defense. Key holdings are engineering and building systems contractor EMCOR Group Inc. (EME), followed by civil construction player Sterling Infrastructure Inc. (STRL) and metal products maker Mueller Industries, Inc. (MLI). This diversified allocation helps keep the performance relatively aligned with the overall sector rather than one industry in particular. That said, the still-reasonable backdrop for infrastructure work, grid upgrades, aerospace demand, and selective manufacturing reshoring offers an interesting upside to companies in the portfolio. At the same time, margin pressures from potential higher transportation costs and tariffs may become a headwind for the whole sector, as always, and SMLF’s holdings are not immune as well.

SMLF’s exposure to technology also looks fairly diversified across sub-industries, though the largest industries, semiconductors and software, are slightly underweight. The main holdings include chip interface and IP licensing player Rambus Inc. (RMBS) and connectivity solutions company Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (CRDO). This sleeve likely offers the most upside, considering the solid demand in data center and broader semiconductor segment. On the software side, names like Procore Technologies, Inc. (PCOR), IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), and DocuSign, Inc. (DOCU) show a mix of more established software players and earlier-stage growth names. Still, the fund’s largest position in the sector is Jabil, a manufacturing and supply-chain solutions provider. This is a portfolio that should do well from here, as market leadership broadens beyond the usual big techs, though volatility is likely to be higher than in the broader market, driven by spending cycles, such as a softer growth period that we are likely to experience in the short term.

In financial services, the largest exposure is to regional banks, through names such as Popular, Inc. (BPOP), East West Bancorp, Inc. (EWBC), and First Citizens BancShares, Inc. (FCNCA). Other core segments, where the fund is overweight, include investment banking and brokerage, with a few holdings like electronic brokerage platform Interactive Brokers and advisory firm Evercore Inc. (EVR), as well as insurance and asset management through Old Republic International Corporation (ORI), Unum Group (UNM), Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (AMG), and Janus Henderson Group plc (JHG). That sector allocation gives the fund a somewhat mixed outlook. Sure, regional banks and brokers can do well as credit conditions stay healthy, supporting loan growth, while higher trading activity also benefits brokerage platforms. But there is no question that the sector remains vulnerable to an economic downturn driven by higher rates to combat inflation, as we can not rule out the potential effect of prolonged tensions in the Middle East yet. This is particularly applicable to smaller banks, which are much more reliant on loan growth than global institutions, which have a more diversified source of revenue, including investment banking and trading.

Beyond these core sectors, the fund’s allocation in healthcare is heavily tilted toward biotechnology, with over 100 holdings, including United Therapeutics and Natera, Inc. (NTRA), leaving the sector performance tied to the companies’ drug pipelines, along with regulatory issues, which are always a key factor in the segment. Meanwhile, holdings in consumer discretionary are fairly diversified, though with overweight exposure to retailers, which increases the sensitivity to consumer spending and economic conditions. In sum, we see here a portfolio with a cyclical bias, which is likely to perform well in the long run, considering the U.S. economy remains healthy in general. However, the short-term outlook is more cloudy, given mixed economic data, including a marginally negative industrial production in March, which tends to hit small caps more directly, as many of these companies are tied to domestic manufacturing.

The sector allocation consensus among small-cap funds appears more balanced than what we typically see in large-cap strategies, where technology is generally by far the largest sector. Within this group, allocations are spread more evenly across industrials, financial services, healthcare, and technology.

In that context, SMLF’s 19.8% allocation to industrials stands out, exceeding most peers such as SCHA (15.9%) and IJR (15.7%), while remaining closer to VB (20.1%). On the other hand, the fund’s 15.0% exposure to financial services falls short of peers like JHSC (18.4%) and FYX (17.8%), which lean more heavily into the sector.

Despite these differences, sector correlations remain relatively high across the group, ranging from 0.90 to 0.98, reflecting a sector mix within small-cap ETFs that does not change much for both market-cap-weighted funds and multi-factor alternatives.

Valuation In Line With The Benchmark

SMLF’s 15.2x P/E represents a slight 3% discount to the Russell 2000’s 15.7x. This small discount reflects a sector positioning where allocation gaps with the index are not representative. On top of that, factors driving valuation down, like overweight exposure to value sectors, like consumer staples, are partially offset by an overweight stance in a premium segment, such as consumer discretionary.

The fund has a modest growth profile. Still, its historical earnings growth of 0.8% is marginally higher than the Russell 2000’s -0.6%, and its 2.6% sales growth also outpaces the benchmark’s 2.2% by a thin 0.4 percentage points. While this picture illustrates a challenging environment over the past few years within the whole small-cap category, SMLF has delivered marginally stronger growth in sectors where it is overweight, such as industrials and consumer staples.

SMLF also surpasses the Russell 2000 on profitability, but by a wider margin. The fund’s EBITDA margin of 18.3% sits about 3.4 percentage points above the Russell 2000’s 14.9%, while its ROE of 10.0% is also well ahead of the benchmark’s roughly -0.3%. This is supported by better profitability across several important sectors, including industrials, consumer discretionary, technology, healthcare, and communication services, which suggests that the profitability factor appears to play a major role in the stock selection process.

In summary, SMLF’s factor-based approach helps the fund exhibit more attractive valuation, growth, and profitability measures. Though indicators are only marginally better, this picture gives the fund a quality tilt. And this is something that matters in small caps in particular, a segment that is more sensitive to the economic outlook amid a mixed macro environment, as mentioned in the previous section, helping partially offset the fund’s cyclical bias, driven by greater exposure to industrials and consumer discretionary.

SMLF trades at the higher end of the peer group’s valuation range, as most peers’ valuations remain at the 14x P/E level. This picture is especially clear among multi-factor peers (JHSC, FYX, and GSSC), as these have a marginally greater exposure to value sectors, such as financial services, consumer staples, and energy. SMLF’s premium valuation relative to peers is also consistent with its market cap allocation mix, as most peers have larger positions in smaller capitalization companies. The heavy exposure of peers like IJR, FYX, and GSSC to micro caps further illustrates this point.

Consistent Outperformance

SMLF’s annualized returns of 18.7% (3-year) and 10.2% (5-year) have outpaced the Russell 2000, driven by consecutive years of strong outperformance over the 2021-2024 period. The fund has also done well relative to peers, with the best total returns over the 5-year window and the second-best performance over the 3-year period, only behind FYX.

On volatility, SMLF has seen marginally more contained price action than the Russell 2000 over the past 3 years, with a beta of 1.26, while it has kept pace with the index over the 5-year stretch, both with a beta of 1.09. Although multi-factor peers (JHSC, FYX, and GSSC) and also IJR have generated slightly lower beta than SMLF over time, a bright spot here for SMLF is that it has shown a tendency to hold up better during market declines than the Russell 2000 and most peers as well, as illustrated by its downside capture ratios of 156 (3-year) and 117 (5-year). That is also evidenced by the fund’s outperformance in the bearish year of 2022, when the Russell 2000 and peers saw much sharper declines.

With an average daily volume of $22.25 million, SMLF is a fairly liquid fund, though its liquidity is clearly lower than larger peers like VB, SCHA, and especially IJR. That results in a 0.13% bid-ask spread that is not as tight as those more liquid funds in the group, but still much narrower than multi-factor peers JHSC and FYX. The fund’s 24% turnover is in the middle of the pack and well below multi-factor peers. This leads to a 0.15% expense ratio that is below those peers, though it remains above market-cap-weighted peers VB, SCHA, and IJR. SMLFs 1.17% trailing 12-month yield is in line with the group, while its tracking error is by far the highest among peers and arguably high for a passive fund. Lastly, SMLF’s Sharpe ratio of 0.61 (3-year) and 0.36 (5-year) is ahead of most funds in the group, which suggests the fund’s factor exposure has added enough excess return to make it an interesting alternative from a risk-return perspective within the small-cap ETF segment.

Upside Drivers and Risk Considerations

SMLF’s forward setup looks fairly balanced. Sure, the fund should benefit as the market continues to broaden beyond large-cap leaders and investors start to lean more heavily into smaller companies. In that sense, a small-cap portfolio that exhibits better profitability, like SMLF, may stand out, as holdings are expected to hold up better in a still uneven economic backdrop. At the same time, the fund’s mix of industrials, selective technology, and consumer discretionary could offer a sizable upside, underscored by earnings growth estimates of over 10% for 2026.

Meanwhile, the obvious concern is SMLF’s cyclical bias. This may become more of a headwind, given recent softness in industrial production and tariff-related uncertainty. The trade policy backdrop under the current administration remains unknown to a certain degree for domestically exposed companies, especially in industrial and consumer-facing segments, where input cost dynamics can impact companies’ bottom lines quickly. This is a headwind that can affect small caps the most, as they usually generate the bulk of their revenue domestically. Financials are another mixed bag, as regional banks’ outlook relies on loan growth to a large extent. In summary, the setup is still constructive, but it needs signs of stable growth and contained inflation. Without that, SMLF may continue to outperform small-cap benchmarks like the Russell 2000, but will probably deliver subdued returns relative to the broader market, including large and mega caps, as these larger players operate more mature and stable businesses that are usually more resilient in a softening growth environment like we are seeing now.

Final Thoughts

SMLF’s multi-factor structure ends up offering a small-cap portfolio that looks slightly better positioned than the Russell 2000 in terms of valuation, profitability, and volatility. Despite the fund’s quality tilt and strong track record in the category, the current outlook is far from ideal for smaller companies, given the mixed picture described in the previous section. That said, although I am not surprised to see SMLF delivering double-digit returns over the next twelve months, this will largely depend on investor sentiment about the category and macroeconomic conditions that confirm the healthy earnings growth projected by analysts. In conclusion, my take on this fund is that it is likely to continue to outperform peers. However, I keep a neutral stance on this fund, as my view is that small caps in general still do not offer the best risk-reward opportunity compared to large and mega caps, as earnings for the S&P 500, the large-cap benchmark, are still expected to grow by double digits in 2026.
